title: The Third Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: March 7, 2021

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Third Sunday in Lent, falling within the penitential season of Lent (beginning Ash Wednesday, February 17, 2021). The RCL readings for this Sunday were Exodus 20:1-17 (the Ten Commandments), Psalm 19, 1 Corinthians 1:18-25, and John 2:13-22 (Jesus cleansing the temple). The temple-cleansing Gospel narrative and the Torah foundation readings would have provided the scriptural framework for any homily on repentance, spiritual renewal, and divine authority during this mid-Lenten period.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the early stages of post-COVID reopening and pandemic response protocols, with many dioceses still operating under strict capacity limitations and hybrid worship models. The Diocese of Colorado under Bishop Kee Sloan was navigating the practical and theological challenges of welcoming parishioners back to in-person worship while managing the Delta variant concerns that would intensify later in 2021. No major General Convention or significant denominational controversies were dominating headlines at this specific moment, though the broader church remained engaged in ongoing discussions about racial justice following the 2020 racial reckoning.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early spring weather with temperatures in the 50s-60s Fahrenheit, typical for early March along the Front Range. The Colorado legislature was in its active session (January-May 2021), debating bills including criminal justice reform and pandemic-related measures. Saint John's Cathedral sits in the Capitol Hill neighborhood, which had experienced significant social unrest in 2020 and was still recovering from the year's protests and business disruptions, though the immediate tension had moderated by March 2021.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The COVID-19 vaccination campaign was accelerating significantly, with President Biden announcing the expansion of vaccine eligibility and the U.S. approaching 100 million doses administered by mid-March. The trial of former police officer Derek Chauvin for the murder of George Floyd was underway in Minneapolis (jury selection began March 8), dominating national news and sustaining attention on racial justice and police reform. Congress was debating the $1.9 trillion American Rescue Plan stimulus package, which passed both chambers by March 10, reflecting ongoing economic anxiety from the pandemic recession.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The European Union was struggling with COVID-19 vaccine rollout delays and supply chain issues, contrasting with the accelerating U.S. campaign. The Myanmar military coup of February 1, 2021 was still generating international headlines as the military detained Aung San Suu Kyi and suppressed protests, with coordinated resistance continuing. Israel-Palestine tensions were beginning to escalate toward the May 2021 conflict, though major violence had not yet erupted at this March date.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American society was in a cautiously optimistic phase—the vaccination campaign offered hope for an end to lockdowns and isolation, yet anxiety about variants and the drawn-out recovery remained palpable. There was fatigue with pandemic restrictions but also persistent grief and trauma from 2020. Culturally, there was heightened awareness of racial justice issues following the Floyd murder trial, and Americans were beginning to imagine post-pandemic life while still inhabiting considerable uncertainty. The 2021 NCAA March Madness tournament was about to begin, offering a return to normal sports spectacle that Denver audiences would welcome.
